hints.Nominative: Nominative plural neuter is the same form, but 'O' signals an exclamatory accusative
hints.Vocative: Vocative is used for direct address of persons — 'tempora' is an abstract noun in exclamatory accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'temporibus' — 'tempora' is accusative plural

hints.1st: 1st declension plural accusative ends in -as — 'tempora' (nom. sing. 'tempus') is 3rd declension neuter
hints.2nd: 2nd declension neuter plural would be -a as well, but 'tempus, temporis' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns have gen. -us — 'tempus, temporis' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural looks the same, but parallel to 'tempora' this is exclamatory accusative with 'O'
hints.Dative: Dative plural would be 'moribus' — 'mores' is accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'moribus' — 'mores' is accusative

hints.1st: 1st declension plural would end in -ae — 'mos, moris' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension plural would end in -i/-os — 'mos, moris' is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension plural would end in -es as well, but 'mos, moris' with gen. in -is is 3rd

steps.prompt: This exclamation contains no verb. What is the grammatical term for this construction?

hints.Ablative absolute: Ablative absolute uses a participle + noun in the ablative — this has no verb or participle at all
hints.Historic infinitive: Historic infinitive replaces a finite verb in narration — there is no infinitive here
hints.Asyndeton: Asyndeton is omission of conjunctions — while 'O … O …' lacks a conjunction, the key feature is the verbless accusative exclamation

steps.prompt: What rhetorical figure is 'O tempora, o mores'?

hints.Chiasmus: Chiasmus is ABBA word order — this is parallel repetition of 'O' at the start of each phrase
hints.Litotes: Litotes is understatement through negation — this is emphatic exclamation, the opposite of understatement
hints.Hendiadys: Hendiadys expresses one idea with two nouns joined by 'and' — 'tempora' and 'mores' are distinct concepts

steps.prompt: What emotion does Cicero express with this exclamation?
steps.target: Moral outrage at the corruption of the times
choices: Moral outrage at the corruption of the times

hints.Joy at Rome's prosperity: The interjection 'O' here is a lament, not celebration — Cicero is denouncing the state of affairs
hints.Nostalgia for his youth: Cicero is not reminiscing — he is condemning the present moral decay that tolerates Catiline
hints.Fear of military defeat: The concern is not military but moral — 'mores' (customs/morals) points to ethical decline

translation: O the times! O the customs!
